skill.name: coverage-analyzer
skill.description: Analyze test coverage reports, identify uncovered code paths, and recommend targeted tests for Python projects.
skill.summary: Find coverage gaps and prioritize the tests needed to reach coverage targets.
skill.author: Atyantik

security_audit.summary: Static high-risk weak-cryptography matches and many Ruby backtick matches are false positives caused by markdown documentation and marketplace text in SKILL.md and output.json. The confirmed risk is legitimate but elevated operational guidance: the skill instructs agents to run project-local test and coverage commands, read local coverage artifacts, write a coverage history file, and optionally install diff-cover.

medium_findings.title: Project-Local Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run local commands such as coverage, test, shell pipeline, and diff-cover workflows. This is expected for a coverage-analysis skill, but project-local Makefile targets and shell pipelines can execute arbitrary code from the checked-out project.
medium_findings.recommendation: Require user confirmation before running commands, inspect Makefile targets first, and avoid executing commands in untrusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and directly related to the skill workflow. The risk is contextual because the commands are legitimate but can execute project-controlled code.

medium_findings.title: Optional Package Installation Command
medium_findings.description: The advanced diff coverage section recommends installing diff-cover. Package installation can contact external package indexes and execute installation hooks, so it should not run automatically.
medium_findings.recommendation: Treat dependency installation as a user-approved action and prefer existing project lock files or documented environment setup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The install command is explicit and commonly uses network access. It is presented as optional workflow guidance, not hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Local File Reading and Coverage History Write
low_findings.description: The skill asks agents to read project documentation, inspect coverage artifacts, and append coverage data to a local history file. This is normal for the stated purpose, but it should remain limited to the active repository.
low_findings.recommendation: Limit reads and writes to repository files needed for coverage analysis and avoid collecting unrelated local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access is explicitly documented and aligned with coverage analysis. No evidence suggests access outside the project workspace.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Matches Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography matches in output.json and SKILL.md are caused by ordinary marketplace text such as coverage, code, and quality descriptions. The reconnaissance matches are documentation examples and testing guidance, not system probing or network scanning behavior.
low_findings.recommendation: No security remediation is required for these dismissed matches; keep generated reports clear that they are documentation artifacts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines shows markdown examples and descriptive content, not executable cryptography or reconnaissance. No hidden code path was found in the two scanned files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Example Test Data
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL appears inside a sample pytest fixture as api_url test data. No fetch, upload, telemetry, or other outbound request is defined at that location.
low_findings.recommendation: No action is required for the example URL. Keep sample domains generic and avoid real service endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is embedded in a sample configuration fixture. There is no surrounding code that performs a network request.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Matches Dismissed
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick detections are markdown inline code and fenced examples, not Ruby execution. Some examples still describe shell commands, which are separately recorded as medium operational risk.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep command examples clearly documented and require user approval before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited syntax is markdown formatting, not executable Ruby. The remaining risk comes from the documented command examples, not from hidden code execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Coverage History Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The historical tracking example uses shell command substitution and a pipeline to append coverage data. This is useful but should be reviewed before execution in untrusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Ask for user approval before running shell pipelines and avoid modifying files unless the user requests persistent history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line contains direct shell command substitution and output redirection. It is documented rather than hidden, which lowers severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dependency Installation from Coverage Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The diff coverage workflow includes an installation command. Installing packages can run code from external dependencies and should be user controlled.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Use the project dependency manager or ask the user before installing any package.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The installation step is explicit. The semantic context is optional setup guidance, not covert package execution.

content.value_statement: Coverage reports show missed lines, but they often do not explain which gaps matter most. This skill ranks uncovered code and recommends targeted tests for reliable coverage improvement.
actual_capabilities: Reviews coverage output and highlights files with uncovered lines.
Ranks gaps by criticality, including error handling and validation paths.
Suggests specific tests, fixtures, and parametrized cases to add.
Compares current coverage against an 80 percent target.
Explains coverage trends and likely quick wins.
Guides branch, module, and diff coverage checks.
limitations: Requires coverage data or permission to run local coverage commands.
Does not prove that high coverage means the code is bug free.
Command examples depend on the project Makefile and test tooling.
Test suggestions still need developer review before implementation.

use_cases.title: Prioritize missing tests before merging
use_cases.description: Find uncovered changed code and choose the smallest useful set of tests before a pull request is merged.

use_cases.title: Review risk in uncovered paths
use_cases.description: Identify untested validation, error handling, and integration paths that may hide production defects.

use_cases.title: Track coverage quality trends
use_cases.description: Monitor whether teams are improving meaningful coverage instead of only increasing line totals.

prompt_templates.title: Review Current Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my current coverage report. Summarize the total coverage, the largest gaps, and the first three tests I should add.

prompt_templates.title: Find Critical Gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Find uncovered error handling, validation, authorization, and edge cases. Rank them by risk and explain why each test matters.

prompt_templates.title: Improve One Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze coverage for this module and suggest targeted tests, fixtures, and parametrized cases that would raise meaningful coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Diff Coverage Recovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare coverage for recent changes against the main branch. Identify regressions, estimate effort, and propose a coverage recovery plan.

output_examples.input: Analyze current test coverage and identify quick wins.
output: Current coverage is below the target by 4.7 percentage points.
The highest risk gap is untested validator error handling.
Two focused tests can cover the main failure paths.
Parser edge cases are a fast second priority.

output_examples.input: Suggest tests for uncovered API retry logic.
output: Add one success-after-retry case and one failure-after-limit case.
Assert the final result, retry count, and raised exception behavior.
Keep external network calls mocked because the retry behavior is the target.

output_examples.input: Explain whether coverage is improving over time.
output: Coverage improved earlier but has stalled in the latest period.
Recent uncovered lines are concentrated in integration and edge paths.
Prioritize changed files first to prevent new regressions.

best_practices: Inspect the project test commands before running coverage in an unfamiliar repository.
Prioritize behavior, edge cases, and failure paths before simple line gains.
Use coverage trends with code review context to avoid misleading conclusions.
anti_patterns: Writing tests that execute lines without meaningful assertions.
Raising coverage by ignoring hard-to-test files instead of reviewing risk.
Running package installation or project commands without user approval.

faq.question: What coverage tools does this skill expect?
faq.answer: It is written for Python projects that use pytest, coverage.py, Makefile commands, and optional diff-cover reports.

faq.question: Can it run coverage commands automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide command usage, but commands should run only with user approval in trusted repositories.

faq.question: Does it generate complete test files?
faq.answer: It recommends tests, fixtures, scenarios, and priorities. Developers should review and adapt the final test code.

faq.question: Why focus on critical paths first?
faq.answer: Critical paths such as validation and error handling often hide defects that line coverage totals do not reveal.

faq.question: Is 80 percent coverage always enough?
faq.answer: No. The skill uses 80 percent as a target, but meaningful coverage and risk reduction matter more.

faq.question: Will it send source code to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence of data exfiltration was found. The skill is documentation-based and uses local project artifacts.
